title: What Is a Function?
objectives: Define a function and identify its domain and range
Use function notation to evaluate functions
Determine whether a relation represents a function
Represent functions using tables, graphs, and formulas

devotional.title: Order in Creation
devotional.content: For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven. Just as God has assigned a purpose to every season, a function assigns exactly one output to every input—reflecting the divine order woven into mathematics.

content.text: Consider a vending machine. You press a button—say, **B3**—and out drops a bag of pretzels. Every time you press **B3**, you get pretzels. Not chips, not a candy bar, not nothing at all: pretzels, always pretzels. Each button corresponds to exactly one product. You would never trust a machine that dispensed a different snack every time you pressed the same button.

This simple expectation—*one input, one definite output*—is the heart of one of the most important ideas in all of mathematics. It is the idea of a **function**, and it will be the language in which we write every theorem, every formula, and every calculation in this course.

Functions are everywhere in daily life, even when we do not call them by name. The scanner at the grocery store assigns a price to each barcode. Your phone's GPS assigns a location to each moment in time. The tax code assigns a tax owed to each level of income. In every case, a rule takes an *input* and produces exactly one *output*.
Let us give this idea a precise name.

content.title: Function
content.content: A **function** $f$ from a set $A$ to a set $B$ is a rule that assigns to each element $x$ in $A$ exactly one element, called $f(x)$, in $B$. The set $A$ is called the **domain** of $f$. The set of all output values $\{f(x) : x \in A\}$ is called the **range** of $f$.

content.text: Two features of this definition deserve emphasis:

items: **Every** element of the domain must have an output. The function cannot "skip" an input.
Each input produces **exactly one** output. A single input cannot yield two different outputs.

content.text: Notice what the definition does *not* require: it does not say that different inputs must produce different outputs. The function $f(x) = x^2$ sends both $3$ and $-3$ to $9$, and that is perfectly acceptable. What matters is that each input has *one* output, not that each output comes from one input.

content.title: Deciding whether a rule is a function
content.problem:
Which of the following rules define functions from the given domain to $\mathbb{R}$?

1. The rule that assigns to each real number $x$ the value $\sqrt{x}$. Domain: $\mathbb{R}$.

2. The rule that assigns to each nonnegative real number $x$ the value $\sqrt{x}$. Domain: $[0, \infty)$.

3. The rule that assigns to each person their biological mother. Domain: all living people.
content.solution:
1. **Not a function** on the stated domain. The input $x = -4$ has no real-number output, so the rule fails to assign a value to every element of $\mathbb{R}$.

2. **Yes, a function.** Every nonnegative number has exactly one nonnegative square root. The domain $[0,\infty)$ has been chosen so that each input produces exactly one output.

3. **Yes, a function.** Every person has exactly one biological mother. (Two people may share the same mother—that is fine; the definition only requires uniqueness of output for each input.)
